narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, looking at factors like grades, test scores, family background, and more to forecast first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 29 (well under the 36.70 average), Year 1 school decile of 3 (lower than the average 5.71), Year 3 decile of 4 (below the 5.53 average), low family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 42% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. Among all the factors considered, your family income background had the strongest influence, actually working in your favor by pushing the prediction toward bar passage, perhaps reflecting how students from similar low-income situations often show resilience and determination. Your Year 3 school decile ranking came next in importance, also helping your case by nudging the model toward expecting you to pass, indicating some academic recovery or stability later in school. However, your Year 1 decile had a notable counteracting effect, pulling the prediction toward failure because it signaled an early weaker performance compared to peers. Overall, while these top influences balanced out somewhat, combined with your below-average LSAT and GPA, they tipped the scales to that concerning 42% failure risk—higher than what the model sees in typical admitted students, who often enter with stronger early grades and test scores. Your full-time status aligns with most applicants (over 90%), so it didn't sway things much either way. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law amid real challenges like family finances—many from similar backgrounds thrive with targeted support. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it toward or above 36, strengthening your undergrad record through post-bac courses if possible, and seeking programs with bar prep resources or holistic reviews that value your unique story. Building a standout personal statement highlighting your resilience could also open doors at schools with more flexible criteria. With deliberate steps, you can reshape this prediction and pursue your legal ambitions successfully.
